Q1: I tried to keep things consistent, usually studying in the evenings after work and on weekends. With limited resources, I had to be strategic about when I could access the internet at the library or community center. I'd plan out my week on Sundays, blocking time for each course, though sometimes life got in the way. Being 27 and having other responsibilities means I can't just study whenever I want like younger students might.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular because I needed that structure to stay on track. I'd study Tuesday and Thursday evenings and most of Saturday mornings without fail. But when deadlines approached, I'd have to squeeze in extra sessions wherever I could. The consistency helped me manage my medium stress levels, even when motivation was low.
Q3: Honestly, I logged in mainly when I needed to check assignment deadlines or access lecture recordings. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about new content and that would prompt me to log in. I'm an auditory learner, so I was always checking if new audio materials or recorded lectures were posted. Fear of missing something important kept me checking regularly.
Q4: After about the third week, logging in became automatic for me, like checking my phone in the morning. I'd open the LMS during my lunch break at work just to see if anything new was posted. It became part of my daily routine because I was scared of falling behind. The routine helped manage my anxiety about the course, even though my overall engagement stayed pretty low.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd play them multiple times, sometimes at different speeds, to really absorb the content. The discussion boards were useful too, even though I only posted twice, because reading other students' questions often clarified things I was confused about. Video content totaling over 244 minutes really helped me grasp difficult concepts.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary reading materials because with my limited resources, I couldn't always access or print them. The interactive quizzes I sometimes delayed because they felt less important than the main assignments. Some of the discussion prompts seemed too theoretical, so I'd skim them rather than engage deeply. Time was precious, so I had to prioritize what directly affected my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could directly see how it connected to my assignments or exams. If I could listen to it while commuting or doing chores, even better. Anything that helped me understand concepts I was struggling with was valuable. I also appreciated activities that didn't require constant internet access since that's a real limitation for me.
Q8: Required activities got my full attention because they affected my grade directly, and I couldn't afford to slip up. Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always indulge in given my time constraints. Sometimes I'd feel guilty skipping optional content, but with 15 hours of study time weekly and two online courses, I had to make tough choices. The fear of missing something important in optional materials did stress me out sometimes.
Q9: Absolutely, I downloaded everything I could when I had good internet access. I'd save lecture recordings to my phone and listen to them on the bus or while cooking. My notes from those audio sessions were extensive, filling several notebooks. I also borrowed textbooks from the library and studied from those without any LMS activity showing.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened offline, honestly. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it four or five times over the following days. My study group met in person at the library where we'd discuss concepts for hours. None of that deep processing showed up in the system, just that initial download. The LMS would show minimal activity during my most productive study periods.
Q11: This was my standard approach due to my limited internet access. I'd batch-download materials on Saturdays when I could get to a reliable connection, then work through them all week. One video might show as watched once, but I'd actually review it multiple times on my phone offline. It was the most efficient way for me to study given my circumstances.
Q12: Around mid-semester, things got really difficult when my work hours increased unexpectedly. I felt scared that I wouldn't be able to keep up with both courses. The CBT sessions helped me work through some of that fear, but there were weeks where I was just surviving, not thriving. My motivation dipped significantly during that period, and even logging in felt like a chore.
Q13: Fatigue was a constant companion, especially balancing work and studies at 27. When I was exhausted, I'd still log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely affected my engagement, sometimes I'd have the LMS open but be mentally elsewhere worrying about bills or family matters. The confusion from some poorly explained concepts made me want to disengage entirely at times.
Q14: Yes, there were definitely times I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered to other worries. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph five times and still not process it because I was too tired or stressed. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through the motions without real learning happening.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts more than anything else because they required me to be articulate and thoughtful, which was hard when I was drained. Some readings I avoided because they seemed overwhelming and I was scared I wouldn't understand them. I knew they were important, but the fear of struggling with difficult content made me procrastinate. The CBT sessions actually helped me recognize this avoidance pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary driver, I won't lie about that. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before an assignment was due. I'd cram in all my watching and reading to prepare for submissions. Without deadlines, I probably would have spread things out more evenly, but the pressure helped me focus despite my low motivation.
Q17: When I got good feedback on my assignments, it gave me a temporary boost in confidence and engagement. Seeing my 93.74 assignment average made me feel like my efforts were paying off. Negative or unclear feedback sometimes made me scared to submit the next assignment, worrying I'd make the same mistakes. I'd often revisit feedback multiple times before starting new work.
Q18: The weekly structure helped me plan my limited study time effectively. Having clear modules with defined content made it easier to batch-download materials. However, when multiple deadlines clustered together, the structure felt suffocating rather than helpful. The 92.6% attendance I maintained was partly because the structure made it clear what I'd miss if I fell behind.
Q19: The availability of recorded lectures made engagement much easier for an auditory learner like me. Having materials that could be downloaded was crucial given my resource limitations. What made it harder was when content required constant internet connection or when readings were only available in formats I couldn't easily save. Discussion requirements with short windows were challenging with my schedule.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something, maybe they're confused and clicking around trying to understand what's expected. They could be like me, downloading materials to study offline, so submissions come later. Or perhaps they're scared and anxious, checking everything repeatedly without feeling confident enough to submit. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some degree. My engagement level shows as low, but I earned a Merit grade because my real learning happened offline. I'd access materials minimally online but study them extensively on my own. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying, but my exam score of 80.38 tells a different story. Efficiency matters more than visible activity.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when students accessed materials and submitted work, the basic mechanics of participation. They capture patterns like my deadline-driven spikes in activity. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, whether I actually understood what I watched or just had it playing. They can't see my notebooks full of handwritten notes or the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just exposing yourself to it. It's asking questions, even if just in your own head, and connecting new concepts to what you already know. For me, engagement looks like listening to a lecture repeatedly until I can explain it to someone else. It's an internal process that doesn't always have external markers.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I often felt about keeping up, or how the CBT sessions helped me manage that fear. It can't capture the resilience I've built over years of studying with limited resources, or how I've learned to maximize every minute of study time. My peer interactions show as only 4, but I have a study group that meets offline regularly. The data misses the whole person behind the numbers.
